serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried silver pomfret in Singapore is one medium fillet weighing approximately 120 g, commonly served as part of a rice-based meal at hawker centres or zi char stalls.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 206 mg/100 g, partially offset by potassium 277 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one medium fillet and avoid adding soy sauce, salt, or high-sodium dipping sauces, as the baseline sodium of 206 mg/100 g is in the borderline range; the potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable for DASH diet adherence but additional sodium from condiments can negate this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein 32.5 g/100 g supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried silver pomfret is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrates and no sugar, producing no glycaemic load; however, patients should avoid sweet marinades, batter coatings, or dipping sauces that add rapidly absorbed carbohydrates, and should be mindful of the higher fat content (22.7 g/100 g) from deep-frying when managing total caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat 22.7 g/100 g from deep-frying; cholesterol 97 mg/100 g; beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from silver pomfret
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Silver pomfret is a naturally omega-3-rich marine fish with cardiovascular benefit, but deep-frying significantly elevates total fat and caloric density; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer steamed or pan-grilled preparations, limit consumption of deep-fried versions to occasional servings, and monitor overall saturated fat intake given the estimated 4.5 g/100 g saturated fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) from silver pomfret reduce joint inflammation; high protein supports tissue repair
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Silver pomfret provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis management; however, deep-frying at high temperatures generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may exacerbate inflammation, so steamed or shallow pan-fried preparations are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried silver pomfret (炸白鲳鱼; Ikan Bawal Putih Goreng) is a popular Singaporean seafood dish featuring the silver pomfret (Pampus argenteus), a prized marine flatfish known for its delicate, sweet-flavoured white flesh and characteristic silvery skin. The fish is typically deep-fried whole or as boneless fillets until the exterior achieves a golden-brown, crispy crust while the interior remains moist, white, and flaky. It is a staple at Singapore hawker centres, zi char stalls, and Chinese seafood restaurants, commonly served alongside steamed rice, stir-fried vegetables, and soup as part of a family-style meal. Nutritionally, fried silver pomfret is energy-dense at 334 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 32.5 g and elevated fat of 22.7 g per 100 g attributable to the deep-frying process. Carbohydrate content is zero, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is 206 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management, while potassium at 277 mg/100 g provides a favourable counterbalance. Cholesterol is 97 mg/100 g. The fish is naturally rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which support cardiovascular and joint health, though deep-frying reduces the omega-3 integrity compared to steamed preparations. Glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. This dish is halal and gluten-free when prepared without batter or wheat-based coatings.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03010017) at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. The food identity is exact and unambiguous.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; silver pomfret contains no carbohydrates in its natural state and deep-frying without batter adds no carbohydrates — confirmed zero per HPB FoodData. sugar_g is naturally zero as this is a plain deep-fried fish with no added sugar. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and full micronutrient panel for fried silver pomfret are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03010017 as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g of 4.5 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pomfret, cooked (NDB 15236) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is below the 5 g/100 g threshold relevant for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease assessments. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g per HPB); the numeric value 0 is used as a machine-readable sentinel consistent with the template requirement that glycemic_index be numeric, with gi_category set to 'not_applicable' to clarify no true GI measurement applies. FoodOn match is broad (fried fish food product); no species-specific FoodOn URI for silver pomfret exists as of 2026-04-16. FoodEx2 code A00LC is an adapted match for pomfret species with fried preparation context.
